---
title: "Explanatory Data Analysis"
subtitle: "Environmental Drivers of Purple Urchin Gonad Index in Central California Kelp Forests"
author: "Sofia Rivas and Jocelyn Rojas"
format: pdf
editor: visual
---

# 1. Introduction and Research Question

Kelp forests are among the most productive and biodiverse marine ecosystems on the Pacific coast, providing critical habitat structure and trophic support for a wide range of species. Within these ecosystems, purple sea urchins (*Strongylocentrotus purpuratus*) have a critical ecological role as kelp grazers. Their grazing behavior can either maintain healthy kelp forests or, under certain conditions, contribute to the formation of urchin barrens. These barren states are characterized by high urchin densities, usually dominated by crustose-coralline algae, and little to no macroalgae cover, representing a fundamentally different and less productive ecosystem state. Gonadosomatic index (GI), the ratio of gonad mass to total body mass, serves as an indicator of urchin condition and reproductive potential. As kelp forest restoration efforts expand along the California coast in response to widespread kelp decline, identifying which habitat characteristics predict urchin gonad condition is essential for prioritizing restoration sites and evaluating intervention success. This project applies a Bayesian principal component regression approach to identify key habitat predictors of purple urchin gonad index across survey sites in central California, with the aim of informing evidence-based kelp forest restoration.

## Research Question

To what extent do biological and environmental variables predict **sea urchin gonad index** across kelp forests and urchin barrens, and which variables are the strongest predictors of gonad index?

# 2. Load Relevant Packages

```{r}
#| output: false
library(tidyverse) #data wrangling (includes other packages)
library(dplyr) #data wrangling 
library(ggplot2) #plotting
library(brms) #Bayesian statistics 
library(bayesplot) #posterior probability distributions 
library(factoextra) #variance plot (scree plot)
library(tidybayes) #helps convert Bayesian modeling for ggplot2 and tidyverse
library(patchwork) #put plots together
```

# 3. Data Wrangling

The data for this project came from a Monterey Bay Aquarium kelp forest recovery research program. From 2024-2025, data was collected during the summer field seasons. Data collected included SCUBA monitoring surveys (recording species density, kelp cover and topography) and purple sea urchin collection from 12 sites (with multiple zones within sites) around Carmel Bay and the Monterey Bay peninsula. Sea urchins were dissected in the lab to calculate mean gonadosomatic index for each zone within sites.

The raw dataset contains \~80 variables with 98 observations. To simplify the dataset for more accurate analysis, we selected 20 variables that we deemed as most relevant to our research question.

```{r}
#raw data
predictors_raw <- readRDS("quad_build3.rds") 

#select relevant columns and delete rows with NA values 
predictors <- predictors_raw %>% 
   mutate(juveniles = macj + nerj + ptej + lsetj + eisj,
         n_macro_plants_m2 = n_macro_plants_20m2/20,
         macro_stipe_density_m2 = macro_stipe_density_20m2/20,
         adults = (density20m2_ptecal + density20m2_eisarb + density20m2_nerlue + density20m2_lamset)/20,
         marine_snails = tegula_densitym2 + pomaulax_densitym2,
         density_cancer = density20m2_cancer_spp/20,
         density_lamstump = density20m2_lamstump/20,
         density_macstump = density20m2_macstump/20) %>% 
  dplyr::select(mean_gi, n_macro_plants_m2, macro_stipe_density_m2, adults,
juveniles, density_cancer, density_lamstump, density_macstump, relief_cm, risk_index, purple_urchin_densitym2, purple_urchin_conceiledm2, red_urchin_densitym2, red_urchin_conceiledm2, marine_snails, lamr, macr, cov_crustose_coralline, cov_desmarestia_spp, cov_lam_holdfast_live, cov_articulated_coralline, cov_fleshy_red, cov_bare_rock, cov_green_algae, cov_barnacle, cov_bare_sand, cov_colonial_tunicate, cov_dictyota_dictyopteris, cov_sponge, cov_dead_kelp_holdfast_any, cov_mac_holdfast_live, cov_red_turf_2_cm, cov_corynactis_californica, cov_dictyoneurum_spp) %>% 
  na.omit()

#final dataset for project 
predictors2 <- predictors %>%
  dplyr::select(mean_gi, cov_mac_holdfast_live, macro_stipe_density_m2, n_macro_plants_m2, cov_bare_sand, purple_urchin_conceiledm2, cov_crustose_coralline, purple_urchin_densitym2, relief_cm, marine_snails, red_urchin_densitym2, cov_corynactis_californica, cov_barnacle, cov_articulated_coralline, cov_desmarestia_spp, cov_colonial_tunicate, density_lamstump, cov_dead_kelp_holdfast_any, cov_dictyoneurum_spp, red_urchin_conceiledm2)
```

# 4. Principal Component Analysis and BRMS Model

## Why we chose PCA

Even though we narrowed our dataset, we still had too many variables for the number of observations to produce a reliable model. To deal with this, we used Principal Component Analysis. PCA is a dimensionality reduction technique used to simplify complex datasets by transforming potentially correlated variables (e.g., kelp stipe density, holdfast cover, macroalgal abundance) into a smaller set of variables, called principal components.

## Step 1: PCA

```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#run PCA: run only on predictor variables, not the response variable (mean_gi)
#standardize variables 
pca <- prcomp(predictors2 %>% 
                dplyr::select(-mean_gi), 
                scale. = TRUE) 

#visualize 
fviz_eig(pca)
```

## Step 2: BRM Model Setup

```{r}
#create matrix of raw PC scores (reference scree plot)
pc_scores <- as.data.frame(pca$x[, 1:10])

#add response variable (mean_gi) into new dataframe
pc_data <- pc_scores %>%
              mutate(mean_gi = predictors2$mean_gi)

#set priors for brm using PCA 
priors_pca <- c(
  prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b"),
  prior(normal(6.5, 2), class = "Intercept"), #mean mean_gi is ~6.5
  prior(student_t(3, 0, 2), class = "sigma"))

#density plot to check for normality of the data
ggplot(pc_data, aes(x = mean_gi)) +
  geom_density(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(x = "Mean GI", y = "Density") +
  theme_classic()

#Q-Q plot as another way to check for normality 
ggplot(pc_data, aes(sample = mean_gi)) +
  stat_qq() +
  stat_qq_line(color = "red") +
  labs(x = "Theoretical quantiles", 
       y = "Sample quantiles",
       title = "Q-Q plot of mean GI") +
  theme_classic()
```

## Step 3: Model Selection

After checking the data's distribution and running the gaussian brm model, we wanted to see if there may be a better distribution family because the distribution is skewed. We then used the posterior predictive checks with pp_check() to visualize the model fit. Then, we re-ran the brm model with only the significant principal components (PCs) from the first gaussian model and used the same process for a skew-normal model. Comparing the model_pca_gaus with the model_pca_skew using loo_compare(), the skew-normal distribution is a better fit for predictive power.

```{r}
#| output: false
#run brm model with default normal/gaussian distribution 
model_pca <- brm(mean_gi ~ .,
                 data = pc_data,
                 family = gaussian(),
                 prior = priors_pca,
                 chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000,
                 cores = 4)
```

```{r}
summary(model_pca) #PC1, PC2, and PC3 are significant!

#posterior predictive check 
pp_check(model_pca) #mostly fits well, doesn't account for bump around x=2
```

```{r}
#| output: false
#re-run with only significant PCs
model_pca_gaus <- brm(mean_gi ~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3,
                                  data = pc_data,
                                  family = gaussian(),
                                  prior = priors_pca,
                                  chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000,
                                  cores = 4)
```

```{r}
pp_check(model_pca_gaus)
```

```{r}
#| output: false
#rerun first model with skew_normal distribution 
model_pca_other <- brm(mean_gi ~ ., 
                   data = pc_data,
                   family = skew_normal(), #changed to skew_normal
                   prior = priors_pca,
                   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000,
                   cores = 4)
```

```{r}
summary(model_pca_other) #again, PC1, PC2, and PC3 are significant!
```

```{r}
#| output: false
#final model 
model_pca_skew <- brm(mean_gi ~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3,  # only significant PCs
                   data = pc_data,
                   family = skew_normal(), 
                   prior = priors_pca,
                   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000,
                   cores = 4)
```

```{r}
summary(model_pca_skew)  
bayes_R2(model_pca_skew) #check R^2

#posterior predictive check 
pp_check(model_pca_skew)

#compare gaussian and skew_normal models --> model_pca_skew seems to be the better model! 
loo_gaussian <- loo(model_pca_gaus, moment_match = TRUE)
loo_skew <- loo(model_pca_skew, reloo = TRUE)
loo_compare(loo_gaussian, loo_skew)
```

# 5. Interpreting and Visualizing Model

Now that we have our model, we can start to interpret and visualize it. Starting with the PCs, we created a for loop to print the variables associated with each significant PC.

## PC Interpretations

PC1, PC2, and PC3 were all significant predictors of purple urchin gonad index in the Bayesian skew-normal model (all 95% CIs excluded zero, Rhats = 1.00).

PC1 was the strongest predictor of purple urchin gonad index (β = 0.80, 95% CI: 0.62–0.96) and represented a gradient from kelp forest to barren-like habitat. Zones scoring high on PC1 were characterized by greater *Macrocystis* holdfast cover, stipe density, macro-algal abundance, and concealed purple urchins (meaning urchins were eating drift kelp rather than live kelp, therefore not contributing to kelp loss). In contrast, zones scoring low on PC1 were characterized by higher crustose-coralline algae cover, marine snail density, exposed red and purple urchin densities, and lower substrate relief, which can be indicators of degraded, barren-like habitat. The positive model coefficient indicates that urchins at kelp forest sites had significantly higher gonad index than those at barren-like sites, consistent with drift kelp being the primary food source supporting urchin gonad development.

PC2 had a negative effect (β = -0.58, 95% CI: -0.82– -0.35), suggesting that sites with higher red urchin density, *Corynactis*, and barnacle cover were associated with lower gonad index compared to sites with articulated coralline algae and *Desmarestia*. PC3 had a moderate positive effect (β = 0.37, 95% CI: 0.13–0.67), where sites with greater algal diversity were associated with higher gonad index relative to sites dominated by *Laminaria* stumps, purple urchin densities, and dead kelp holdfasts.

Together, these three habitat gradients explained 41% of variation in purple urchin gonad index (Bayesian R² = 0.41, 95% CI: 0.32–0.49).

Also, PC1, PC2, and PC3 captured 23%, 12%, and 10% of the total variation in habitat conditions (variables) respectively, together summarizing 45% of the overall habitat structure across sites.

```{r}
#what variables are included in PC1, PC2, and PC3?
for(pc in c("PC1","PC2","PC3")) { #starts a for loop 
  cat("\n---", pc, "---\n") #syntax for output: headers for each PC
  cat("TOP POSITIVE:\n") #syntax for output: label for the variables with  largest positive loadings for the current PC
  print(head(sort(pca$rotation[,pc], decreasing = TRUE), 5)) #sorts loadings from largest to smallest, keeps top 5 variables 
  cat("TOP NEGATIVE:\n") #syntax for output: label for the variables with  largest negative loadings for the current PC
  print(head(sort(pca$rotation[,pc], decreasing = FALSE), 5))} #sorts loadings from largest to smallest, keeps top 5 variables 

# % habitat structure variance for each PC
summary(pca)$importance[2, 1:3] * 100 # % variance for each PC 
```

## Visualizations

The coefficient plot visually presents the three significant PCs and their relative effects on mean GI (basically visualizes the summary table). AS we can see, PC1 is furthest from x=0, meaning it has the highest effect (and positive).

```{r}
#density curves of each PC showing posterior distributions 
mcmc_areas(model_pca_skew,
           pars = vars(starts_with("b_"), -b_Intercept), #plot regression coefficients and exclude intercept 
           prob = 0.95, #shade the 95% credible interval 
           prob_outer = 1.0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") + #dotted line at x=0 for visual assessment 
  theme_classic()

```

To show how well the model can predict gonad index, we extracted the posterior mean predictions (fitted values) and 95% credible intervals from the model for each observation. We then plot the predictions against the observed mean gonad index values. Each point represents one survey zone within a site, with horizontal error bars showing prediction uncertainty, and the red dashed line representing the ideal predictions. The model does a fairly okay job at predicting mean GI, but because our model accounts for only about 41% of variation in mean GI, the points will not be very close to the line.

```{r}
pc_data$fitted <- fitted(model_pca_skew)[, "Estimate"] #posterior mean prediction for each observation
pc_data$lower  <- fitted(model_pca_skew)[, "Q2.5"] #lower 95% credible interval
pc_data$upper  <- fitted(model_pca_skew)[, "Q97.5"] #upper 95% credible interval

#plot observed vs predicted to see how well the three ecologically meaningful PCs predict gonad index
ggplot(pc_data, aes(x = fitted/100, y = mean_gi/100)) + #turn units to %
  geom_point(color = "#466995") + #each dot is one observation 
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = lower/100, xmax = upper/100), 
                 alpha = 0.2, 
                 linewidth = 0.3) + #horizontal error bars showing prediction uncertainty
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") + #prediction line 
  labs(x = "Predicted Mean GI (%)", y = "Observed Mean GI (%)") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0,0.15)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0,0.2)) +
  theme_classic()
```

The PCA variable plot shows how the variables relate to PC1 and PC2. Arrows point to the right show varaibles correlated to each other and associated with higher mean gonad index (PC1, β = 0.80), such as \# of *Macrocystis* plants, cover of live *Macrocystis* holdfasts, and concealed purple urchins. Arrows to the left indicate the opposite, and are negatively correlated to GI. Arrows pointing upwards are associated with lower mean gonad index (PC2, β = -0.58), such as bare sand cover, crustose-coralline algae cover, and red urchin density. The gradient of colors represents the strength of contribution, so dark blue variables/arrows are the most influential.

The three individual plots are marginal effect plots, showing the relationships between each PC and mean GI. This visualizes each PCs effect on mean GI. As we can see, the observed points are scattered quite a bit in each plot, but the points follow the line best in the PC1 plot.

```{r}
#plotting PC1 and PC2
fviz_pca_var(pca, axes = c(1, 2), #specify which PCs to plot
             select.var = list(contrib = 10),  #only top # contributors for clear visuals 
             col.var = "contrib",
             repel = TRUE,
               labelsize = 2,
             gradient.cols = c("grey80", "steelblue", "darkblue")) +
  labs(title = "PCA variable plot: PC1 and PC2",
       x = "PC1 (22.7%)",
       y = "PC2 (11.9%)",
       color = "Contribution (%)")+
  theme_classic()

#Rework data to plot PCs separately 
ce <- conditional_effects(model_pca_skew)

# Extract the data from conditional effects
ce_pc1 <- as.data.frame(ce[["PC1"]])
ce_pc2 <- as.data.frame(ce[["PC2"]])
ce_pc3 <- as.data.frame(ce[["PC3"]])

# Build plots from scratch
p1 <- ggplot(ce_pc1, aes(x = PC1, y = estimate__)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower__, ymax = upper__), 
              fill = "lightblue", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue") +
  geom_point(data = pc_data, aes(x = PC1, y = mean_gi),
             color = "#466995", alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(x = "PC1",
       y = "Mean GI (%)") +
  theme_classic()

p2 <- ggplot(ce_pc2, aes(x = PC2, y = estimate__)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower__, ymax = upper__),
              fill = "lightblue", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue") +
  geom_point(data = pc_data, aes(x = PC2, y = mean_gi),
             color = "#466995", alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(x = "PC2",
       y = "Mean GI (%)") +
  theme_classic()

p3 <- ggplot(ce_pc3, aes(x = PC3, y = estimate__)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower__, ymax = upper__),
              fill = "lightblue", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue") +
  geom_point(data = pc_data, aes(x = PC3, y = mean_gi),
             color = "#466995", alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(x = "PC3",
       y = "Mean GI (%)") +
  theme_classic()

# Combine
p1 + p2 + p3
```

# 6. Conclusion

This project demonstrated that habitat structure is a significant predictor of purple urchin gonad index across survey sites in central California, with three principal habitat gradients collectively explaining 41% of variation in gonad condition (Bayesian R² = 0.41, 95% CI: 0.32–0.49). PC1 (representing kelp forest structure) emerged as the strongest predictor, with sites characterized by greater *Macrocystis* holdfast cover, stipe density, and macroalgal plant abundance associated with significantly higher gonad index. PC2 had a significant negative effect, indicating that exposed, barren-like sites with high red urchin density and low structural complexity were associated with poorer gonad condition. PC3 had a moderate positive effect, suggesting that sites with greater macroalgal diversity supported better urchin condition relative to sites dominated by purple urchins and dead kelp remnants. Together, these findings suggest that gonad condition may reflect the broader ecological state of a site, and that habitat-based variables may serve as useful indicators for predicting urchin condition. For restoration practitioners, these results highlight the importance of restoring not just urchin density but the broader habitat conditions that support urchin condition, and suggest that sites with intact kelp structure and high algal diversity are likely to support urchins with greater reproductive potential and higher gonad quality.
